主楼层有三个区。
我们先从那边开始，
从一区开始，然后一路横着过来。
在主楼层这边，
如果你们举手，
那位
负责话筒的工作人员
就会把话筒递给你。
我们会尽量避免同一个区里的同一个人重复提问，
在该区每个人都有机会先问一个问题之前，不会让同一个人再问。
所以你问过一次以后，就把机会留给别人。
到楼座区时，我们那边有一位工作人员，
阳台上则有三位，现在那边基本已经坐满了。
而我们会
在那上面，我们会很感谢
如果你们能离开自己的座位，
走到拿话筒的人那里，
这样无论在楼座还是
在阳台，处理起来都会更方便一些。
如果你们能提前一点过去，
这样如果前面已经有两三个人排队，
你们也可以排队等着提问，
无论是在楼座还是阳台都一样。
所以，
不管你们想问什么，
如果你想听乐观一点的回答，
当然就把问题问给查理。
如果你想听更现实一点的答案，那就问我。
那我们就从一区开始吧。
有时候我们从这里看得不是很清楚。
事实上，我现在连监视器都看不见。
那边有人吗？
如果提问前能先说一下你的名字和来自哪里，我们会很感激。
我叫 Michael Mullen，来自奥马哈。
能否请你们谈谈对衍生品的看法？
我注意到戴尔电脑的股价周五因为衍生品亏损跌了两个半点。
这个问题是关于衍生品的。
今天这个房间里，正好有写过这方面最佳文章的人。
大约一个月前，《财富》杂志上 Carol Loomis 写过一篇关于衍生品的文章，写得非常非常好，是目前为止最好的一篇。
我们现场还有一些来自所罗门、从事衍生品业务的人。
这是个非常宽泛的话题。
正如我们去年说的，我想当时有人问，90 年代最大的金融故事会是什么。
我们当时说，我们当然不知道，但如果非要选一个主题，那很可能就是衍生品。
原因在于，它们很容易被用来加上不同寻常规模的杠杆，
而且有时候连参与其中的人都没有完全理解它们。
只要你把无知和借来的钱放在一起，
就会产生相当有意思的后果，
尤其是当数字变得很大时。
最近宝洁公司的公告就让你们看到了这一点，
至于宝洁那些衍生品的细节，我并不清楚，但据我至少从
媒体报道中了解到，原本只是利率互换的东西，最后却变成了宝洁背上了看跌期权头寸。
chapters.transcript_en:
WARREN BUFFETT: Zone 7.
AUDIENCE MEMBER: Mr. Buffett, my name's Allan Maxwell from Omaha. I've got two questions.
What is your next goal in life now that you're the richest man in the country?
WARREN BUFFETT: That's easy. It’s to be the oldest man in the country. (Laughter and applause)
AUDIENCE MEMBER: Secondly, you talk about good management with corporations and that you try and buy companies with good management.
I feel that I have about as much chance of meeting good managers, other than yourself, as I do bringing Richard Nixon back to life.
How do I, as an average investor, find out what good management is?
WARREN BUFFETT: Well, I think you judge management by two yardsticks.
One is how well they run the business and I think you can learn a lot about that by reading about both what they've accomplished and what their competitors have accomplished, and seeing how they have allocated capital over time.
You have to have some understanding of the hand they were dealt when they themselves got a chance to play the hand.
But, if you understand something about the business they’re in — and you can’t understand it in every business, but you can find industries or companies where you can understand it — then you simply want to look at how well they have been doing in playing the hand, essentially, that's been dealt with them.
And then the second thing you want to figure out is how well that they treat their owners. And I think you can get a handle on that, oftentimes. A lot of times you can't.  I mean it — they’re many companies that obviously fall in — somewhere — in that 20th to 80th percentile and it's a little hard to pick out where they do fall.
But, I think you can usually figure out — I mean, it's not hard to figure out that, say, Bill Gates, or Tom Murphy, or Don Keough, or people like that, are really outstanding managers. And it's not hard to figure out who they're working for.
And I can give you some cases on the other end of the spectrum, too.
It's interesting how often the ones that, in my view, are the poor managers also turn out to be the ones that really don't think that much about the shareholders, too.  The two often go hand in hand.
But, I think reading of reports — reading of competitors’ reports — I think you'll get a fix on that in some cases. You don't have to — you know, you don't have to make a hundred correct judgments in this business or 50 correct judgments. You only have to make a few. And that's all we try to do.
And, generally speaking, the conclusions I've come to about managers have really come about the same way you can make yours.  I mean they come about by reading reports rather than any intimate personal knowledge or — and knowing them personally at all.
So it — you know, read the proxy statements, see what they think of — see how they treat themselves versus how they treat the shareholders, look at what they have accomplished, considering what the hand was that they were dealt when they took over compared to what is going on in the industry.
And I think you can figure it out sometimes. You don't have to figure out very often.
Charlie?
CHARLIE MUNGER: Nothing to add.
WARREN BUFFETT: Ok, we're back to zone 1.
AUDIENCE MEMBER: Hi there. My name is Lee. I'm from Palo Alto, California.
In meeting Ajit Jain, I've been very impressed over the years. And I think I even met his parents once they came from India.
Please comment on your deepest impressions of his personality and managerial skills, and also how you go about exactly keeping somebody who has such fine skills within the fold. He might go to Walt Disney someday and, you know, pull down 200 million.
WARREN BUFFETT: Well, if he gets offered 200 million — (laughs) — we may not compete too vigorously at that level. (Laughter)
We basically try to run a business so that — Charlie and I have two jobs.  We have to identify and keep good managers interested after we’ve figured out who they are.
And that often is a little different here, because I would say a majority of our managers are financially independent, so that they don't go to work because they are worried about putting kids through school or putting food on the table. So they have to have some reason to go to work aside from that.
They have to be treated fairly in terms of compensation, but they also have to figure it is better than playing golf every day or whatever it may be.
And, so that's one of the jobs we have and we basically attack that the same way — we look at what they do the same way we look at what we do.
We've got a wonderful group of shareholders. Before I ran this, I had a partnership. I had a great group of partners. And essentially, I like to be left alone to do what I did. I like to be judged on the scorecard at the end of the year rather than on every stroke, and not second guessed in a way that was inappropriate.
I like to have people who understood the environment in which I was operating.
And so the important thing we do with managers, generally, is to find the .400 hitters and then not tell them how to swing, as I put in the report.
The second thing we do is allocate capital. And aside from that, we play bridge. (Laughter)
Pretty much what happens at Berkshire.
So, with any of the managers you might name here, we try to make it interesting and fun for them to run their business. We try to have a compensation arrangement that's appropriate for the kind of business they’re in.
We have no company-wide compensation plan. We wouldn't dream of having some compensation expert or consultant come in and screw it up. (Laughter)
We try to — some businesses require a lot of capital that we're in, some require no capital. Some are easy businesses where good profit margins are a cinch to come by, but we're really paying for the extra beyond that. Some are very tough businesses to make money in.
And it would be crazy to have some huge framework that we try to place everybody in that — where one size would fit all.
People, generally, are compensated relating in some manner that relates to how their business does as opposed to — there's no reason to pay anybody based on how Berkshire does, because no one has responsibility for Berkshire except for Charlie and me.
And we try to make them responsible for their own units, compensated based on how those units do.
We try to understand the businesses they’re in, so we know what the difference between a good performance and a bad performance —
And that's about — that's how we work with people.
We've had terrific luck over the years in retaining the managers that we wanted to retain. I think, largely, it's because — particularly if they sell us a business — to a great extent, the next day they're running it just as they were the day before. And they're having as much fun running their business as I am running Berkshire.
Charlie?
CHARLIE MUNGER: Well, I've got nothing to add, but I think that concept of treating the other fellow the way you'd like to be treated if the roles were reversed — it's so simple, when you stop to think about it, but —
It's a rare evening when Ajit and Warren aren't talking once on the phone. It's more than a business relationship, at least it seems that way to me.
WARREN BUFFETT: Yeah, well, it is. It will stay that way, too.
CHARLIE MUNGER: And by the way, we like our businesses — our relationships — to be more than a business relationship
WARREN BUFFETT: Charlie and I are very — we basically — it's a luxury but it's a luxury that we should try to nurture — we get to work with people we like. And it makes life a lot simpler.
It probably helps in that goal of being the oldest living American, too. (Laughter)
CHARLIE MUNGER:  Yeah, and we tend to like people we admire. (Laughter)
WARREN BUFFETT: Yeah, who do we like that we don't admire, Charlie? (Laughter)
Start naming names. These people have names. (Laughter)
WARREN BUFFETT: Zone 2.
AUDIENCE MEMBER: My name is Peter Bevelin from Sweden.
How do you perceive Guinness long-term, economics growth-wise?
WARREN BUFFETT: Fitz — would you repeat that please, Fitz. What was it?  What firm growth-wise?
VOICE: Guinness.
AUDIENCE MEMBER: Guinness.
WARREN BUFFETT: Oh, Guinness.
I'm not as much of an expert on Guinness' products as Charlie is.
CHARLIE MUNGER: We approved that. (Laughter)
WARREN BUFFETT: You didn't hear him. He said, "I approved that."
I made the decision to buy Guinness and Guinness has — it's down somewhat from — actually, the price in pounds is about the same but the pound is at about $1.46 or -7 against an average of $1.80-something, so we've had a significant exchange loss on that.
The — Guinness' — despite the name — you know, the main product, of course, is scotch. And that's where most of the money is made, although they make good money in brewing.
But, distilling is the main business. And, you know, the usage of scotch, particularly in this country, the trends have not been strong at all, but that was true when we bought it, too.
There are some countries around the world where it's grown and there are certain countries where it's a huge prestige item.
I mean, in certain parts of the Far East, the more you pay for scotch, the better you think people think of you. Which I don't understand completely, but I hope it continues. (Laughter)

transcript_lines.en: WARREN BUFFETT: Zone 7.

transcript_lines.en: I mean, in certain parts of the Far East, the more you pay for scotch, the better you think people think of you. Which I don't understand completely, but I hope it continues. (Laughter)

chapters.transcript_zh:
包括本次会议现场投下的票数，
将交由公司秘书存入本次会议记录。
谢谢你，Fitzsimmons 先生。
沃伦·巴菲特、苏珊·T·巴菲特、霍华德·G·巴菲特、马尔科姆·G·蔡斯三世、查尔斯·T·芒格和小沃尔特·斯科特已当选董事。
在我们休会之前，还有其他事项需要提交大会处理吗？
如果没有，我请 Walter Scott Jr. 先生再提出一项动议。
我提议会议休会。
附议？
休会议案已经提出。
也已获得附议，我们将进行口头表决。
还有讨论吗？
如果没有，赞成的请说“赞成”。
反对的请说“不赞成”。
会议休会。
这就是美国中部的民主。
我想向大家介绍一些让这里顺利运转的人。
请大家把掌声留到最后，因为今天到场的经理人相当多。
我也不确定哪些人一定在场。
有些人可能还在外面照看店里的生意。
首先，来自内布拉斯加家具城的 Louie、Ron 和 Irv Blumkin。
我不确定谁在场，但请在场的 Blumkin 家族成员站一下，好吗？
好，看起来 Irv 在，我不太看得清。
接下来是 Borsheim's 的 Susan Jock。
Susan？
她在那儿。
Susan 昨天创下了创纪录的一天。
Susan 几个月前才刚接任 CEO，现在已经在刷新纪录了。
继续保持。
来自 Central
States Indemnity 的，我们有
Kaiser 一家。
我不确定今天来了哪些人，
但有 Bill Sr.、Bill Jr.、
John 和 Dick。
Kaiser 家的人，请站起来。
我想我看见他们了。
National Indemnity 的 Don Worcester。
Rod Eldred，来自
Home State Companies。
来自 Cypress 的 Brad Kinsler，
我们那家工伤赔偿公司。
阿吉特·贾因——我们在东部承保大额保单的人——以及 Mike Goldberg，他负责我们的房地产
融资业务，也总体统筹保险业务。Mike。
还有 Gary Heldman（Fechheimer's）、Chuck Huggins（See's，那位糖果先生），以及 Stan Lipsey，来自
《布法罗新闻报》。
顺便说一句，Chuck 跟我们在一起已经二十多年了。
Stan 跟我共事也已经超过 25 年了。
来自 HH Brown 的 Frank Rooney 和 Jim Isler。
来自 Precision Steel 的 Dave Hillstrom。
来自 Scott Fetzer 的 Ralph Schey。
Peter Lundner，来自我们最新收购的 Dexter Shoe。
他的合伙人 Harold Alfond 今天没法到场，因为他太太生病了。
最后，是跟查理和我共事时间最长的一位经理人，Harry Bottle，来自
K&W。
Harry，你在吗？
就在那儿。
那就是 Harry。
对。
早在 1962 年前后，Harry 可是救了我们一命，那时我一时发疯，跑去做风车
生意，结果 Harry 把我从里面救了出来。
这就是我们的经理团队，也请大家给他们一点掌声。
我还有一个关于明年的好消息要告诉大家，
除了我们会搬到更大的场地之外。
未来几个月，这里将新增从纽约、华盛顿
和洛杉矶直飞的航班，由 Midwest Express 提供。
所以我希望他们这条线运营得非常成功，
也希望这样能让大家来这座城市更方便。
现在接下来的
在接下来的
两个小时零
大约 15 分钟里，我们会有一个环节，
专门接受大家提问。
我们一共分成七个区，
主楼层有三个区。
我们先从那边开始，
从一区开始，然后一路横着过来。
在主楼层这边，
如果你们举手，
那位
负责话筒的工作人员
就会把话筒递给你。
我们会尽量避免同一个区里的同一个人重复提问，
在该区每个人都有机会先问一个问题之前，不会让同一个人再问。
所以你问过一次以后，就把机会留给别人。
到楼座区时，我们那边有一位工作人员，
阳台上则有三位，现在那边基本已经坐满了。
而我们会
在那上面，我们会很感谢
如果你们能离开自己的座位，
走到拿话筒的人那里，
这样无论在楼座还是
在阳台，处理起来都会更方便一些。
如果你们能提前一点过去，
这样如果前面已经有两三个人排队，
你们也可以排队等着提问，
无论是在楼座还是阳台都一样。
所以，
不管你们想问什么，
如果你想听乐观一点的回答，
当然就把问题问给查理。
如果你想听更现实一点的答案，那就问我。
那我们就从一区开始吧。
有时候我们从这里看得不是很清楚。
事实上，我现在连监视器都看不见。
那边有人吗？
如果提问前能先说一下你的名字和来自哪里，我们会很感激。
我叫 Michael Mullen，来自奥马哈。
能否请你们谈谈对衍生品的看法？
我注意到戴尔电脑的股价周五因为衍生品亏损跌了两个半点。
这个问题是关于衍生品的。
今天这个房间里，正好有写过这方面最佳文章的人。
大约一个月前，《财富》杂志上 Carol Loomis 写过一篇关于衍生品的文章，写得非常非常好，是目前为止最好的一篇。
我们现场还有一些来自所罗门、从事衍生品业务的人。
这是个非常宽泛的话题。
正如我们去年说的，我想当时有人问，90 年代最大的金融故事会是什么。
我们当时说，我们当然不知道，但如果非要选一个主题，那很可能就是衍生品。
原因在于，它们很容易被用来加上不同寻常规模的杠杆，
而且有时候连参与其中的人都没有完全理解它们。
只要你把无知和借来的钱放在一起，
就会产生相当有意思的后果，
尤其是当数字变得很大时。
最近宝洁公司的公告就让你们看到了这一点，
至于宝洁那些衍生品的细节，我并不清楚，但据我至少从
媒体报道中了解到，原本只是利率互换的东西，最后却变成了宝洁背上了看跌期权头寸。
chapters.transcript_en:
WARREN BUFFETT: Zone 3.
AUDIENCE MEMBER: Good morning. My name is Howard Bask (PH) and I’m from Kansas City. And I've got a theoretical value question for you.
If you were to buy a business and you bought it at its intrinsic value, what's the minimum after-tax free cash flow yield you’d need to get?
WARREN BUFFETT: Well, your question is if we were buying all of a business and we're buying at what we thought was intrinsic value, what was the minimum —
AUDIENCE MEMBER: Correct.
WARREN BUFFETT: — present earning power or what the present — the minimum discount rate of future streams?
AUDIENCE MEMBER: No, what's the minimum current after-tax free cash flow yield you’d…
WARREN BUFFETT: We could conceivably buy a business — I don't think we would be likely to — but we could we could conceivably buy a business that had no current after-tax cash flow. But, we would have to think it had a tremendous future.
But we would not find — obviously the current figures, particularly in the kind of businesses we buy, tend to be representative, we think, of what's going to happen in the future.
But that would not necessarily have to be the case. You can argue, for example, in buying stocks, we bought GEICO at a time when it was losing significant money. We didn't expect it to continue to lose significant money.
But if we think the present value of the future earning power is attractive enough compared to the purchase price, we would not be overwhelmed by what the first year’s figure would be.
Charlie, you want to add to that?
CHARLIE MUNGER: Yeah. We don't care what we report in the first year or two of — after buying anything.
AUDIENCE MEMBER OFF MIC: (INAUDIBLE) on average over the years (INAUDIBLE).
WARREN BUFFETT: Well, I would say that in a world of 7 percent long-term bond rates that we would certainly want to think we were discounting future after-tax streams of cash at at least a 10 percent rate.
But that will depend on the certainty we feel about the business. The more certain we feel about a business, the closer we are willing to play it.
We have to feel pretty certain about any business before we're even interested at all. But there are still degrees of certainty, and —
If we thought we were getting a stream of cash over the next 30 years that we felt extremely certain about, we would use a discount rate that would be somewhat less than if it was one where we thought we might get some surprises in five or 10 years — possibility existed. Charlie?
CHARLIE MUNGER: Nothing to add.
WARREN BUFFETT: OK. Zone 4.
AUDIENCE MEMBER: Maurus Spence from Omaha, Nebraska.
You've made comments on several occasions about the intrinsic business value of the insurance operations. And in this year's report you state that the insurance business possesses an intrinsic value that exceeds book value by a large amount, larger, in fact, than is the case at any Berkshire — other Berkshire business.
I was wondering if you would explain in greater detail why you believe that to be true.
WARREN BUFFETT: Well, I — it's very hard to quantify, as we've said many times in the report. But, I think that it's clear that even taking fairly pessimistic assumptions, that the excess of intrinsic value over carrying value is higher, by some margin, for the insurance business.
And I think that the table in the report that shows you what our cost to float has been over the years, and also what the trend of float has been over the years, would, unless you thought that table had no validity for the future, I think that that table would tend to the point you in the direction of saying the insurance business does have a very significant excess of intrinsic value over carrying value.
Very hard number to put something on. But — and you don't want to extrapolate that table out. But I think that table shows that we started with maybe 20 million of float and that we're up to something close to three billion of float. And that that float has come to us at a cost that's extremely attractive, on average, over the years.
And just to pick an example, last year, when we actually had an underwriting profit, the value of that float was something over $200 million.  And that figure was a lot bigger than it was 10 years ago or 20 years ago.
So that's — that is a stream — last year was unusually favorable, but that is a — that's a very significant stream of earnings, and it's one we feel we have reasonably good prospects in. So we feel very good about the insurance business.
WARREN BUFFETT: OK. Zone 5?
AUDIENCE MEMBER: My name is Cy Rademacher (PH) from Omaha.
Is there any point at which your stock would rise to the point where you might split the stock?
WARREN BUFFETT: Surprise, surprise. (Laughter)
I think I'll let Charlie answer that this year. (Laughs)
He's so popular with the shareholders that I can afford to let him take the tough questions. (Laughter)
CHARLIE MUNGER: I think the answer is no. (Laughter and applause)
I think the idea of carving ownerships in an enterprise into little, tiny $20 pieces is almost insane.  And it's quite inefficient to service a $20 account and I don't see why there shouldn't be a minimum as a condition of joining some enterprise.  Certainly we’d all feel that way if we were organizing a private enterprise.
WARREN BUFFETT: Yeah, we would not carve it up in $20 units.
We find it very — it's interesting because every company finds a way to fill up its common shareholder list. And you can start with the As and work through to the Zs and you’ll — every company in the New York Stock Exchange, one way or another, has attracted some constituency of shareholders.
And frankly, we can't imagine a better constituency than is in this room.  I mean, we have — we don't think we can improve on this group, and we followed certain policies that we think attracted certain types of shareholders and actually pushed away others.
And that is part of our eugenics program here at Berkshire. (Laughter)
CHARLIE MUNGER: Yeah, just look around this room and as you mingle with one another. This is a very outstanding group of people. And why would anybody want a different kind of a group?
WARREN BUFFETT: Yeah, if we cause — if we follow some policies that cause a whole bunch of people to buy Berkshire for the wrong reason, the only way they can buy it is to replace somebody in this room, or in this larger metaphorical room, of shareholders that we have.
So someone in one of these seats gets up and somebody else walks in. The question is do we have a better audience?
I don't think so. So I think that — I think Charlie said it very well.
WARREN BUFFETT: Zone 6.
AUDIENCE MEMBER: Mr. Buffett, my name is Rob Na (PH) and I'm from Omaha, Nebraska.
My question is, given the recent announcement of Midwest Express and their nonstop jet service between East and West Coasts, will this cut down on your use of "The Indefensible?"  (Laughter)
And will you use more commercial air travel?
WARREN BUFFETT: This is a question planted by Charlie. (Laughter)
I think you should know, I take it to the drugstore at the moment, and I — (Laughter)
No, it’s just a question when I start sleeping in it at the hangar.
Nothing will cut back on "The Indefensible."  It’s being painted right now, but I told them to make it last a long time.
Charlie, though, was pointing out the merits of other kinds of transportation last night at the meeting of our managers. He might want to repeat those here.
CHARLIE MUNGER: Well, I just pointed out that the back of the plane arrived at the same time as the front of the plane, invariably.  (Laughter)
WARREN BUFFETT: He's even more of an authority on buses, incidentally, if anybody has his — (Laughter)
WARREN BUFFETT: Zone 7.
AUDIENCE MEMBER: Mr. Buffett, my name's Allan Maxwell from Omaha. I've got two questions.
What is your next goal in life now that you're the richest man in the country?
WARREN BUFFETT: That's easy. It’s to be the oldest man in the country. (Laughter and applause)
AUDIENCE MEMBER: Secondly, you talk about good management with corporations and that you try and buy companies with good management.
I feel that I have about as much chance of meeting good managers, other than yourself, as I do bringing Richard Nixon back to life.
How do I, as an average investor, find out what good management is?
WARREN BUFFETT: Well, I think you judge management by two yardsticks.
One is how well they run the business and I think you can learn a lot about that by reading about both what they've accomplished and what their competitors have accomplished, and seeing how they have allocated capital over time.
You have to have some understanding of the hand they were dealt when they themselves got a chance to play the hand.
But, if you understand something about the business they’re in — and you can’t understand it in every business, but you can find industries or companies where you can understand it — then you simply want to look at how well they have been doing in playing the hand, essentially, that's been dealt with them.
And then the second thing you want to figure out is how well that they treat their owners. And I think you can get a handle on that, oftentimes. A lot of times you can't.  I mean it — they’re many companies that obviously fall in — somewhere — in that 20th to 80th percentile and it's a little hard to pick out where they do fall.
But, I think you can usually figure out — I mean, it's not hard to figure out that, say, Bill Gates, or Tom Murphy, or Don Keough, or people like that, are really outstanding managers. And it's not hard to figure out who they're working for.
And I can give you some cases on the other end of the spectrum, too.
It's interesting how often the ones that, in my view, are the poor managers also turn out to be the ones that really don't think that much about the shareholders, too.  The two often go hand in hand.
But, I think reading of reports — reading of competitors’ reports — I think you'll get a fix on that in some cases. You don't have to — you know, you don't have to make a hundred correct judgments in this business or 50 correct judgments. You only have to make a few. And that's all we try to do.
And, generally speaking, the conclusions I've come to about managers have really come about the same way you can make yours.  I mean they come about by reading reports rather than any intimate personal knowledge or — and knowing them personally at all.
So it — you know, read the proxy statements, see what they think of — see how they treat themselves versus how they treat the shareholders, look at what they have accomplished, considering what the hand was that they were dealt when they took over compared to what is going on in the industry.
And I think you can figure it out sometimes. You don't have to figure out very often.
Charlie?
CHARLIE MUNGER: Nothing to add.
WARREN BUFFETT: Ok, we're back to zone 1.
AUDIENCE MEMBER: Hi there. My name is Lee. I'm from Palo Alto, California.
In meeting Ajit Jain, I've been very impressed over the years. And I think I even met his parents once they came from India.
Please comment on your deepest impressions of his personality and managerial skills, and also how you go about exactly keeping somebody who has such fine skills within the fold. He might go to Walt Disney someday and, you know, pull down 200 million.
WARREN BUFFETT: Well, if he gets offered 200 million — (laughs) — we may not compete too vigorously at that level. (Laughter)
We basically try to run a business so that — Charlie and I have two jobs.  We have to identify and keep good managers interested after we’ve figured out who they are.
And that often is a little different here, because I would say a majority of our managers are financially independent, so that they don't go to work because they are worried about putting kids through school or putting food on the table. So they have to have some reason to go to work aside from that.
They have to be treated fairly in terms of compensation, but they also have to figure it is better than playing golf every day or whatever it may be.
And, so that's one of the jobs we have and we basically attack that the same way — we look at what they do the same way we look at what we do.
We've got a wonderful group of shareholders. Before I ran this, I had a partnership. I had a great group of partners. And essentially, I like to be left alone to do what I did. I like to be judged on the scorecard at the end of the year rather than on every stroke, and not second guessed in a way that was inappropriate.
I like to have people who understood the environment in which I was operating.
And so the important thing we do with managers, generally, is to find the .400 hitters and then not tell them how to swing, as I put in the report.
The second thing we do is allocate capital. And aside from that, we play bridge. (Laughter)
Pretty much what happens at Berkshire.
So, with any of the managers you might name here, we try to make it interesting and fun for them to run their business. We try to have a compensation arrangement that's appropriate for the kind of business they’re in.
We have no company-wide compensation plan. We wouldn't dream of having some compensation expert or consultant come in and screw it up. (Laughter)
We try to — some businesses require a lot of capital that we're in, some require no capital. Some are easy businesses where good profit margins are a cinch to come by, but we're really paying for the extra beyond that. Some are very tough businesses to make money in.
And it would be crazy to have some huge framework that we try to place everybody in that — where one size would fit all.
People, generally, are compensated relating in some manner that relates to how their business does as opposed to — there's no reason to pay anybody based on how Berkshire does, because no one has responsibility for Berkshire except for Charlie and me.
And we try to make them responsible for their own units, compensated based on how those units do.
We try to understand the businesses they’re in, so we know what the difference between a good performance and a bad performance —
And that's about — that's how we work with people.
We've had terrific luck over the years in retaining the managers that we wanted to retain. I think, largely, it's because — particularly if they sell us a business — to a great extent, the next day they're running it just as they were the day before. And they're having as much fun running their business as I am running Berkshire.
Charlie?
CHARLIE MUNGER: Well, I've got nothing to add, but I think that concept of treating the other fellow the way you'd like to be treated if the roles were reversed — it's so simple, when you stop to think about it, but —
It's a rare evening when Ajit and Warren aren't talking once on the phone. It's more than a business relationship, at least it seems that way to me.
WARREN BUFFETT: Yeah, well, it is. It will stay that way, too.
CHARLIE MUNGER: And by the way, we like our businesses — our relationships — to be more than a business relationship
WARREN BUFFETT: Charlie and I are very — we basically — it's a luxury but it's a luxury that we should try to nurture — we get to work with people we like. And it makes life a lot simpler.
It probably helps in that goal of being the oldest living American, too. (Laughter)
CHARLIE MUNGER:  Yeah, and we tend to like people we admire. (Laughter)
WARREN BUFFETT: Yeah, who do we like that we don't admire, Charlie? (Laughter)
Start naming names. These people have names. (Laughter)
WARREN BUFFETT: Zone 2.
AUDIENCE MEMBER: My name is Peter Bevelin from Sweden.
How do you perceive Guinness long-term, economics growth-wise?
WARREN BUFFETT: Fitz — would you repeat that please, Fitz. What was it?  What firm growth-wise?
VOICE: Guinness.
AUDIENCE MEMBER: Guinness.
WARREN BUFFETT: Oh, Guinness.
I'm not as much of an expert on Guinness' products as Charlie is.
CHARLIE MUNGER: We approved that. (Laughter)
WARREN BUFFETT: You didn't hear him. He said, "I approved that."
I made the decision to buy Guinness and Guinness has — it's down somewhat from — actually, the price in pounds is about the same but the pound is at about $1.46 or -7 against an average of $1.80-something, so we've had a significant exchange loss on that.
The — Guinness' — despite the name — you know, the main product, of course, is scotch. And that's where most of the money is made, although they make good money in brewing.
But, distilling is the main business. And, you know, the usage of scotch, particularly in this country, the trends have not been strong at all, but that was true when we bought it, too.
There are some countries around the world where it's grown and there are certain countries where it's a huge prestige item.
I mean, in certain parts of the Far East, the more you pay for scotch, the better you think people think of you. Which I don't understand completely, but I hope it continues. (Laughter)

transcript_lines.zh: 这就是美国中部的民主。
transcript_lines.en: WARREN BUFFETT: Well, I would say that in a world of 7 percent long-term bond rates that we would certainly want to think we were discounting future after-tax streams of cash at at least a 10 percent rate.

transcript_lines.zh: 我想向大家介绍一些让这里顺利运转的人。
transcript_lines.en: But that will depend on the certainty we feel about the business. The more certain we feel about a business, the closer we are willing to play it.

transcript_lines.zh: 请大家把掌声留到最后，因为今天到场的经理人相当多。
transcript_lines.en: We have to feel pretty certain about any business before we're even interested at all. But there are still degrees of certainty, and —

transcript_lines.zh: 我也不确定哪些人一定在场。
transcript_lines.en: If we thought we were getting a stream of cash over the next 30 years that we felt extremely certain about, we would use a discount rate that would be somewhat less than if it was one where we thought we might get some surprises in five or 10 years — possibility existed. Charlie?

transcript_lines.zh: 有些人可能还在外面照看店里的生意。
transcript_lines.en: CHARLIE MUNGER: Nothing to add.

transcript_lines.zh: 首先，来自内布拉斯加家具城的 Louie、Ron 和 Irv Blumkin。
transcript_lines.en: WARREN BUFFETT: OK. Zone 4.

transcript_lines.zh: 我不确定谁在场，但请在场的 Blumkin 家族成员站一下，好吗？
transcript_lines.en: AUDIENCE MEMBER: Maurus Spence from Omaha, Nebraska.

transcript_lines.zh: 好，看起来 Irv 在，我不太看得清。
transcript_lines.en: You've made comments on several occasions about the intrinsic business value of the insurance operations. And in this year's report you state that the insurance business possesses an intrinsic value that exceeds book value by a large amount, larger, in fact, than is the case at any Berkshire — other Berkshire business.

transcript_lines.zh: 接下来是 Borsheim's 的 Susan Jock。
transcript_lines.en: I was wondering if you would explain in greater detail why you believe that to be true.

transcript_lines.zh: Susan？
transcript_lines.en: WARREN BUFFETT: Well, I — it's very hard to quantify, as we've said many times in the report. But, I think that it's clear that even taking fairly pessimistic assumptions, that the excess of intrinsic value over carrying value is higher, by some margin, for the insurance business.

transcript_lines.zh: 她在那儿。
transcript_lines.en: And I think that the table in the report that shows you what our cost to float has been over the years, and also what the trend of float has been over the years, would, unless you thought that table had no validity for the future, I think that that table would tend to the point you in the direction of saying the insurance business does have a very significant excess of intrinsic value over carrying value.

transcript_lines.zh: Susan 昨天创下了创纪录的一天。
transcript_lines.en: Very hard number to put something on. But — and you don't want to extrapolate that table out. But I think that table shows that we started with maybe 20 million of float and that we're up to something close to three billion of float. And that that float has come to us at a cost that's extremely attractive, on average, over the years.

transcript_lines.zh: Susan 几个月前才刚接任 CEO，现在已经在刷新纪录了。
transcript_lines.en: And just to pick an example, last year, when we actually had an underwriting profit, the value of that float was something over $200 million.  And that figure was a lot bigger than it was 10 years ago or 20 years ago.

transcript_lines.zh: 继续保持。
transcript_lines.en: So that's — that is a stream — last year was unusually favorable, but that is a — that's a very significant stream of earnings, and it's one we feel we have reasonably good prospects in. So we feel very good about the insurance business.

transcript_lines.zh: 来自 Central
transcript_lines.en: WARREN BUFFETT: OK. Zone 5?

transcript_lines.zh: States Indemnity 的，我们有
transcript_lines.en: AUDIENCE MEMBER: My name is Cy Rademacher (PH) from Omaha.

transcript_lines.zh: Kaiser 一家。
transcript_lines.en: Is there any point at which your stock would rise to the point where you might split the stock?

transcript_lines.zh: 我不确定今天来了哪些人，
transcript_lines.en: WARREN BUFFETT: Surprise, surprise. (Laughter)

transcript_lines.zh: 但有 Bill Sr.、Bill Jr.、
transcript_lines.en: I think I'll let Charlie answer that this year. (Laughs)

transcript_lines.zh: John 和 Dick。
transcript_lines.en: He's so popular with the shareholders that I can afford to let him take the tough questions. (Laughter)

transcript_lines.zh: Kaiser 家的人，请站起来。
transcript_lines.en: CHARLIE MUNGER: I think the answer is no. (Laughter and applause)

transcript_lines.zh: 我想我看见他们了。
transcript_lines.en: I think the idea of carving ownerships in an enterprise into little, tiny $20 pieces is almost insane.  And it's quite inefficient to service a $20 account and I don't see why there shouldn't be a minimum as a condition of joining some enterprise.  Certainly we’d all feel that way if we were organizing a private enterprise.

transcript_lines.zh: National Indemnity 的 Don Worcester。
transcript_lines.en: WARREN BUFFETT: Yeah, we would not carve it up in $20 units.

transcript_lines.zh: Rod Eldred，来自
transcript_lines.en: We find it very — it's interesting because every company finds a way to fill up its common shareholder list. And you can start with the As and work through to the Zs and you’ll — every company in the New York Stock Exchange, one way or another, has attracted some constituency of shareholders.

transcript_lines.zh: Home State Companies。
transcript_lines.en: And frankly, we can't imagine a better constituency than is in this room.  I mean, we have — we don't think we can improve on this group, and we followed certain policies that we think attracted certain types of shareholders and actually pushed away others.

transcript_lines.zh: 来自 Cypress 的 Brad Kinsler，
transcript_lines.en: And that is part of our eugenics program here at Berkshire. (Laughter)

transcript_lines.zh: 我们那家工伤赔偿公司。
transcript_lines.en: CHARLIE MUNGER: Yeah, just look around this room and as you mingle with one another. This is a very outstanding group of people. And why would anybody want a different kind of a group?

transcript_lines.zh: 阿吉特·贾因——我们在东部承保大额保单的人——以及 Mike Goldberg，他负责我们的房地产
transcript_lines.en: WARREN BUFFETT: Yeah, if we cause — if we follow some policies that cause a whole bunch of people to buy Berkshire for the wrong reason, the only way they can buy it is to replace somebody in this room, or in this larger metaphorical room, of shareholders that we have.

transcript_lines.zh: 融资业务，也总体统筹保险业务。Mike。
transcript_lines.en: So someone in one of these seats gets up and somebody else walks in. The question is do we have a better audience?

transcript_lines.zh: 还有 Gary Heldman（Fechheimer's）、Chuck Huggins（See's，那位糖果先生），以及 Stan Lipsey，来自
transcript_lines.en: I don't think so. So I think that — I think Charlie said it very well.

transcript_lines.zh: 《布法罗新闻报》。
transcript_lines.en: WARREN BUFFETT: Zone 6.

transcript_lines.zh: 顺便说一句，Chuck 跟我们在一起已经二十多年了。
transcript_lines.en: AUDIENCE MEMBER: Mr. Buffett, my name is Rob Na (PH) and I'm from Omaha, Nebraska.

transcript_lines.zh: Stan 跟我共事也已经超过 25 年了。
transcript_lines.en: My question is, given the recent announcement of Midwest Express and their nonstop jet service between East and West Coasts, will this cut down on your use of "The Indefensible?"  (Laughter)

transcript_lines.zh: 来自 HH Brown 的 Frank Rooney 和 Jim Isler。
transcript_lines.en: And will you use more commercial air travel?

transcript_lines.zh: 来自 Precision Steel 的 Dave Hillstrom。
transcript_lines.en: WARREN BUFFETT: This is a question planted by Charlie. (Laughter)

transcript_lines.zh: 来自 Scott Fetzer 的 Ralph Schey。
transcript_lines.en: I think you should know, I take it to the drugstore at the moment, and I — (Laughter)

transcript_lines.zh: Peter Lundner，来自我们最新收购的 Dexter Shoe。
transcript_lines.en: No, it’s just a question when I start sleeping in it at the hangar.

transcript_lines.zh: 他的合伙人 Harold Alfond 今天没法到场，因为他太太生病了。
transcript_lines.en: Nothing will cut back on "The Indefensible."  It’s being painted right now, but I told them to make it last a long time.

transcript_lines.zh: 最后，是跟查理和我共事时间最长的一位经理人，Harry Bottle，来自
transcript_lines.en: Charlie, though, was pointing out the merits of other kinds of transportation last night at the meeting of our managers. He might want to repeat those here.

transcript_lines.zh: K&W。
transcript_lines.en: CHARLIE MUNGER: Well, I just pointed out that the back of the plane arrived at the same time as the front of the plane, invariably.  (Laughter)

transcript_lines.zh: Harry，你在吗？
transcript_lines.en: WARREN BUFFETT: He's even more of an authority on buses, incidentally, if anybody has his — (Laughter)

transcript_lines.zh: 就在那儿。
transcript_lines.en: WARREN BUFFETT: Zone 7.

transcript_lines.zh: 那就是 Harry。
transcript_lines.en: AUDIENCE MEMBER: Mr. Buffett, my name's Allan Maxwell from Omaha. I've got two questions.

transcript_lines.zh: 对。
transcript_lines.en: What is your next goal in life now that you're the richest man in the country?

transcript_lines.zh: 早在 1962 年前后，Harry 可是救了我们一命，那时我一时发疯，跑去做风车
transcript_lines.en: WARREN BUFFETT: That's easy. It’s to be the oldest man in the country. (Laughter and applause)

transcript_lines.zh: 生意，结果 Harry 把我从里面救了出来。
transcript_lines.en: AUDIENCE MEMBER: Secondly, you talk about good management with corporations and that you try and buy companies with good management.

transcript_lines.zh: 这就是我们的经理团队，也请大家给他们一点掌声。
transcript_lines.en: I feel that I have about as much chance of meeting good managers, other than yourself, as I do bringing Richard Nixon back to life.

transcript_lines.zh: 我还有一个关于明年的好消息要告诉大家，
transcript_lines.en: How do I, as an average investor, find out what good management is?

transcript_lines.zh: 除了我们会搬到更大的场地之外。
transcript_lines.en: WARREN BUFFETT: Well, I think you judge management by two yardsticks.

transcript_lines.zh: 未来几个月，这里将新增从纽约、华盛顿
transcript_lines.en: One is how well they run the business and I think you can learn a lot about that by reading about both what they've accomplished and what their competitors have accomplished, and seeing how they have allocated capital over time.

transcript_lines.zh: 和洛杉矶直飞的航班，由 Midwest Express 提供。
transcript_lines.en: You have to have some understanding of the hand they were dealt when they themselves got a chance to play the hand.

transcript_lines.zh: 所以我希望他们这条线运营得非常成功，
transcript_lines.en: But, if you understand something about the business they’re in — and you can’t understand it in every business, but you can find industries or companies where you can understand it — then you simply want to look at how well they have been doing in playing the hand, essentially, that's been dealt with them.

transcript_lines.zh: 也希望这样能让大家来这座城市更方便。
transcript_lines.en: And then the second thing you want to figure out is how well that they treat their owners. And I think you can get a handle on that, oftentimes. A lot of times you can't.  I mean it — they’re many companies that obviously fall in — somewhere — in that 20th to 80th percentile and it's a little hard to pick out where they do fall.

transcript_lines.zh: 现在接下来的
transcript_lines.en: But, I think you can usually figure out — I mean, it's not hard to figure out that, say, Bill Gates, or Tom Murphy, or Don Keough, or people like that, are really outstanding managers. And it's not hard to figure out who they're working for.

transcript_lines.zh: 在接下来的
transcript_lines.en: And I can give you some cases on the other end of the spectrum, too.

transcript_lines.zh: 两个小时零
transcript_lines.en: It's interesting how often the ones that, in my view, are the poor managers also turn out to be the ones that really don't think that much about the shareholders, too.  The two often go hand in hand.

transcript_lines.zh: 大约 15 分钟里，我们会有一个环节，
transcript_lines.en: But, I think reading of reports — reading of competitors’ reports — I think you'll get a fix on that in some cases. You don't have to — you know, you don't have to make a hundred correct judgments in this business or 50 correct judgments. You only have to make a few. And that's all we try to do.

transcript_lines.zh: 专门接受大家提问。
transcript_lines.en: And, generally speaking, the conclusions I've come to about managers have really come about the same way you can make yours.  I mean they come about by reading reports rather than any intimate personal knowledge or — and knowing them personally at all.

transcript_lines.zh: 我们一共分成七个区，
transcript_lines.en: So it — you know, read the proxy statements, see what they think of — see how they treat themselves versus how they treat the shareholders, look at what they have accomplished, considering what the hand was that they were dealt when they took over compared to what is going on in the industry.

transcript_lines.zh: 主楼层有三个区。
transcript_lines.en: And I think you can figure it out sometimes. You don't have to figure out very often.

transcript_lines.zh: 我们先从那边开始，
transcript_lines.en: Charlie?

transcript_lines.zh: 从一区开始，然后一路横着过来。
transcript_lines.en: CHARLIE MUNGER: Nothing to add.

transcript_lines.zh: 在主楼层这边，
transcript_lines.en: WARREN BUFFETT: Ok, we're back to zone 1.

transcript_lines.zh: 如果你们举手，
transcript_lines.en: AUDIENCE MEMBER: Hi there. My name is Lee. I'm from Palo Alto, California.

transcript_lines.zh: 那位
transcript_lines.en: In meeting Ajit Jain, I've been very impressed over the years. And I think I even met his parents once they came from India.

transcript_lines.zh: 负责话筒的工作人员
transcript_lines.en: Please comment on your deepest impressions of his personality and managerial skills, and also how you go about exactly keeping somebody who has such fine skills within the fold. He might go to Walt Disney someday and, you know, pull down 200 million.

transcript_lines.zh: 就会把话筒递给你。
transcript_lines.en: WARREN BUFFETT: Well, if he gets offered 200 million — (laughs) — we may not compete too vigorously at that level. (Laughter)

transcript_lines.zh: 我们会尽量避免同一个区里的同一个人重复提问，
transcript_lines.en: We basically try to run a business so that — Charlie and I have two jobs.  We have to identify and keep good managers interested after we’ve figured out who they are.

transcript_lines.zh: 在该区每个人都有机会先问一个问题之前，不会让同一个人再问。
transcript_lines.en: And that often is a little different here, because I would say a majority of our managers are financially independent, so that they don't go to work because they are worried about putting kids through school or putting food on the table. So they have to have some reason to go to work aside from that.

transcript_lines.zh: 所以你问过一次以后，就把机会留给别人。
transcript_lines.en: They have to be treated fairly in terms of compensation, but they also have to figure it is better than playing golf every day or whatever it may be.

transcript_lines.zh: 到楼座区时，我们那边有一位工作人员，
transcript_lines.en: And, so that's one of the jobs we have and we basically attack that the same way — we look at what they do the same way we look at what we do.

transcript_lines.zh: 阳台上则有三位，现在那边基本已经坐满了。
transcript_lines.en: We've got a wonderful group of shareholders. Before I ran this, I had a partnership. I had a great group of partners. And essentially, I like to be left alone to do what I did. I like to be judged on the scorecard at the end of the year rather than on every stroke, and not second guessed in a way that was inappropriate.

transcript_lines.zh: 而我们会
transcript_lines.en: I like to have people who understood the environment in which I was operating.

transcript_lines.zh: 在那上面，我们会很感谢
transcript_lines.en: And so the important thing we do with managers, generally, is to find the .400 hitters and then not tell them how to swing, as I put in the report.

transcript_lines.zh: 如果你们能离开自己的座位，
transcript_lines.en: The second thing we do is allocate capital. And aside from that, we play bridge. (Laughter)

transcript_lines.zh: 走到拿话筒的人那里，
transcript_lines.en: Pretty much what happens at Berkshire.

transcript_lines.zh: 这样无论在楼座还是
transcript_lines.en: So, with any of the managers you might name here, we try to make it interesting and fun for them to run their business. We try to have a compensation arrangement that's appropriate for the kind of business they’re in.

transcript_lines.zh: 在阳台，处理起来都会更方便一些。
transcript_lines.en: We have no company-wide compensation plan. We wouldn't dream of having some compensation expert or consultant come in and screw it up. (Laughter)

transcript_lines.zh: 如果你们能提前一点过去，
transcript_lines.en: We try to — some businesses require a lot of capital that we're in, some require no capital. Some are easy businesses where good profit margins are a cinch to come by, but we're really paying for the extra beyond that. Some are very tough businesses to make money in.

transcript_lines.zh: 这样如果前面已经有两三个人排队，
transcript_lines.en: And it would be crazy to have some huge framework that we try to place everybody in that — where one size would fit all.

transcript_lines.zh: 你们也可以排队等着提问，
transcript_lines.en: People, generally, are compensated relating in some manner that relates to how their business does as opposed to — there's no reason to pay anybody based on how Berkshire does, because no one has responsibility for Berkshire except for Charlie and me.

transcript_lines.zh: 无论是在楼座还是阳台都一样。
transcript_lines.en: And we try to make them responsible for their own units, compensated based on how those units do.

transcript_lines.zh: 所以，
transcript_lines.en: We try to understand the businesses they’re in, so we know what the difference between a good performance and a bad performance —

transcript_lines.zh: 不管你们想问什么，
transcript_lines.en: And that's about — that's how we work with people.

transcript_lines.zh: 如果你想听乐观一点的回答，
transcript_lines.en: We've had terrific luck over the years in retaining the managers that we wanted to retain. I think, largely, it's because — particularly if they sell us a business — to a great extent, the next day they're running it just as they were the day before. And they're having as much fun running their business as I am running Berkshire.

transcript_lines.zh: 当然就把问题问给查理。
transcript_lines.en: Charlie?

transcript_lines.zh: 如果你想听更现实一点的答案，那就问我。
transcript_lines.en: CHARLIE MUNGER: Well, I've got nothing to add, but I think that concept of treating the other fellow the way you'd like to be treated if the roles were reversed — it's so simple, when you stop to think about it, but —

transcript_lines.zh: 那我们就从一区开始吧。
transcript_lines.en: It's a rare evening when Ajit and Warren aren't talking once on the phone. It's more than a business relationship, at least it seems that way to me.

transcript_lines.zh: 有时候我们从这里看得不是很清楚。
transcript_lines.en: WARREN BUFFETT: Yeah, well, it is. It will stay that way, too.

transcript_lines.zh: 事实上，我现在连监视器都看不见。
transcript_lines.en: CHARLIE MUNGER: And by the way, we like our businesses — our relationships — to be more than a business relationship

transcript_lines.zh: 那边有人吗？
transcript_lines.en: WARREN BUFFETT: Charlie and I are very — we basically — it's a luxury but it's a luxury that we should try to nurture — we get to work with people we like. And it makes life a lot simpler.

transcript_lines.zh: 如果提问前能先说一下你的名字和来自哪里，我们会很感激。
transcript_lines.en: It probably helps in that goal of being the oldest living American, too. (Laughter)

transcript_lines.zh: 我叫 Michael Mullen，来自奥马哈。
transcript_lines.en: CHARLIE MUNGER:  Yeah, and we tend to like people we admire. (Laughter)

transcript_lines.zh: 能否请你们谈谈对衍生品的看法？
transcript_lines.en: WARREN BUFFETT: Yeah, who do we like that we don't admire, Charlie? (Laughter)

transcript_lines.zh: 我注意到戴尔电脑的股价周五因为衍生品亏损跌了两个半点。
transcript_lines.en: Start naming names. These people have names. (Laughter)

transcript_lines.zh: 这个问题是关于衍生品的。
transcript_lines.en: WARREN BUFFETT: Zone 2.

transcript_lines.zh: 今天这个房间里，正好有写过这方面最佳文章的人。
transcript_lines.en: AUDIENCE MEMBER: My name is Peter Bevelin from Sweden.

transcript_lines.zh: 大约一个月前，《财富》杂志上 Carol Loomis 写过一篇关于衍生品的文章，写得非常非常好，是目前为止最好的一篇。
transcript_lines.en: How do you perceive Guinness long-term, economics growth-wise?

transcript_lines.zh: 我们现场还有一些来自所罗门、从事衍生品业务的人。
transcript_lines.en: WARREN BUFFETT: Fitz — would you repeat that please, Fitz. What was it?  What firm growth-wise?

transcript_lines.zh: 这是个非常宽泛的话题。
transcript_lines.en: VOICE: Guinness.

transcript_lines.zh: 正如我们去年说的，我想当时有人问，90 年代最大的金融故事会是什么。
transcript_lines.en: AUDIENCE MEMBER: Guinness.

transcript_lines.zh: 我们当时说，我们当然不知道，但如果非要选一个主题，那很可能就是衍生品。
transcript_lines.en: WARREN BUFFETT: Oh, Guinness.

transcript_lines.zh: 原因在于，它们很容易被用来加上不同寻常规模的杠杆，
transcript_lines.en: I'm not as much of an expert on Guinness' products as Charlie is.

transcript_lines.zh: 而且有时候连参与其中的人都没有完全理解它们。
transcript_lines.en: CHARLIE MUNGER: We approved that. (Laughter)

transcript_lines.zh: 只要你把无知和借来的钱放在一起，
transcript_lines.en: WARREN BUFFETT: You didn't hear him. He said, "I approved that."

transcript_lines.zh: 就会产生相当有意思的后果，
transcript_lines.en: I made the decision to buy Guinness and Guinness has — it's down somewhat from — actually, the price in pounds is about the same but the pound is at about $1.46 or -7 against an average of $1.80-something, so we've had a significant exchange loss on that.

transcript_lines.zh: 尤其是当数字变得很大时。
transcript_lines.en: The — Guinness' — despite the name — you know, the main product, of course, is scotch. And that's where most of the money is made, although they make good money in brewing.

transcript_lines.zh: 最近宝洁公司的公告就让你们看到了这一点，
transcript_lines.en: But, distilling is the main business. And, you know, the usage of scotch, particularly in this country, the trends have not been strong at all, but that was true when we bought it, too.

transcript_lines.zh: 至于宝洁那些衍生品的细节，我并不清楚，但据我至少从
transcript_lines.en: There are some countries around the world where it's grown and there are certain countries where it's a huge prestige item.

transcript_lines.zh: 媒体报道中了解到，原本只是利率互换的东西，最后却变成了宝洁背上了看跌期权头寸。
transcript_lines.en: I mean, in certain parts of the Far East, the more you pay for scotch, the better you think people think of you. Which I don't understand completely, but I hope it continues. (Laughter)

专门接受大家提问。
我们一共分成七个区，
主楼层有三个区。
我们先从那边开始，
从一区开始，然后一路横着过来。
在主楼层这边，

就会把话筒递给你。
我们会尽量避免同一个区里的同一个人重复提问，
在该区每个人都有机会先问一个问题之前，不会让同一个人再问。

而我们会

如果你们能离开自己的座位，
走到拿话筒的人那里，
这样无论在楼座还是
在阳台，处理起来都会更方便一些。
如果你们能提前一点过去，

不管你们想问什么，
如果你想听乐观一点的回答，
当然就把问题问给查理。
如果你想听更现实一点的答案，那就问我。
那我们就从一区开始吧。
有时候我们从这里看得不是很清楚。
事实上，我现在连监视器都看不见。
那边有人吗？
如果提问前能先说一下你的名字和来自哪里，我们会很感激。
我叫 Michael Mullen，来自奥马哈。
能否请你们谈谈对衍生品的看法？
我注意到戴尔电脑的股价周五因为衍生品亏损跌了两个半点。
这个问题是关于衍生品的。
今天这个房间里，正好有写过这方面最佳文章的人。
大约一个月前，《财富》杂志上 Carol Loomis 写过一篇关于衍生品的文章，写得非常非常好，是目前为止最好的一篇。
我们现场还有一些来自所罗门、从事衍生品业务的人。
这是个非常宽泛的话题。
正如我们去年说的，我想当时有人问，90 年代最大的金融故事会是什么。
我们当时说，我们当然不知道，但如果非要选一个主题，那很可能就是衍生品。
原因在于，它们很容易被用来加上不同寻常规模的杠杆，
而且有时候连参与其中的人都没有完全理解它们。
只要你把无知和借来的钱放在一起，
就会产生相当有意思的后果，
尤其是当数字变得很大时。
最近宝洁公司的公告就让你们看到了这一点，
至于宝洁那些衍生品的细节，我并不清楚，但据我至少从
媒体报道中了解到，原本只是利率互换的东西，最后却变成了宝洁背上了看跌期权头寸。
chapters.transcript_en:
WARREN BUFFETT: Zone 6.
AUDIENCE MEMBER: Mr. Buffett, my name is Rob Na (PH) and I'm from Omaha, Nebraska.
My question is, given the recent announcement of Midwest Express and their nonstop jet service between East and West Coasts, will this cut down on your use of "The Indefensible?"  (Laughter)
And will you use more commercial air travel?
WARREN BUFFETT: This is a question planted by Charlie. (Laughter)
I think you should know, I take it to the drugstore at the moment, and I — (Laughter)
No, it’s just a question when I start sleeping in it at the hangar.
Nothing will cut back on "The Indefensible."  It’s being painted right now, but I told them to make it last a long time.
Charlie, though, was pointing out the merits of other kinds of transportation last night at the meeting of our managers. He might want to repeat those here.
CHARLIE MUNGER: Well, I just pointed out that the back of the plane arrived at the same time as the front of the plane, invariably.  (Laughter)
WARREN BUFFETT: He's even more of an authority on buses, incidentally, if anybody has his — (Laughter)
WARREN BUFFETT: Zone 7.
AUDIENCE MEMBER: Mr. Buffett, my name's Allan Maxwell from Omaha. I've got two questions.
What is your next goal in life now that you're the richest man in the country?
WARREN BUFFETT: That's easy. It’s to be the oldest man in the country. (Laughter and applause)
AUDIENCE MEMBER: Secondly, you talk about good management with corporations and that you try and buy companies with good management.
I feel that I have about as much chance of meeting good managers, other than yourself, as I do bringing Richard Nixon back to life.
How do I, as an average investor, find out what good management is?
WARREN BUFFETT: Well, I think you judge management by two yardsticks.
One is how well they run the business and I think you can learn a lot about that by reading about both what they've accomplished and what their competitors have accomplished, and seeing how they have allocated capital over time.
You have to have some understanding of the hand they were dealt when they themselves got a chance to play the hand.
But, if you understand something about the business they’re in — and you can’t understand it in every business, but you can find industries or companies where you can understand it — then you simply want to look at how well they have been doing in playing the hand, essentially, that's been dealt with them.
And then the second thing you want to figure out is how well that they treat their owners. And I think you can get a handle on that, oftentimes. A lot of times you can't.  I mean it — they’re many companies that obviously fall in — somewhere — in that 20th to 80th percentile and it's a little hard to pick out where they do fall.
But, I think you can usually figure out — I mean, it's not hard to figure out that, say, Bill Gates, or Tom Murphy, or Don Keough, or people like that, are really outstanding managers. And it's not hard to figure out who they're working for.
And I can give you some cases on the other end of the spectrum, too.
It's interesting how often the ones that, in my view, are the poor managers also turn out to be the ones that really don't think that much about the shareholders, too.  The two often go hand in hand.
But, I think reading of reports — reading of competitors’ reports — I think you'll get a fix on that in some cases. You don't have to — you know, you don't have to make a hundred correct judgments in this business or 50 correct judgments. You only have to make a few. And that's all we try to do.
And, generally speaking, the conclusions I've come to about managers have really come about the same way you can make yours.  I mean they come about by reading reports rather than any intimate personal knowledge or — and knowing them personally at all.
So it — you know, read the proxy statements, see what they think of — see how they treat themselves versus how they treat the shareholders, look at what they have accomplished, considering what the hand was that they were dealt when they took over compared to what is going on in the industry.
And I think you can figure it out sometimes. You don't have to figure out very often.
Charlie?
CHARLIE MUNGER: Nothing to add.
WARREN BUFFETT: Ok, we're back to zone 1.
AUDIENCE MEMBER: Hi there. My name is Lee. I'm from Palo Alto, California.
In meeting Ajit Jain, I've been very impressed over the years. And I think I even met his parents once they came from India.
Please comment on your deepest impressions of his personality and managerial skills, and also how you go about exactly keeping somebody who has such fine skills within the fold. He might go to Walt Disney someday and, you know, pull down 200 million.
WARREN BUFFETT: Well, if he gets offered 200 million — (laughs) — we may not compete too vigorously at that level. (Laughter)
We basically try to run a business so that — Charlie and I have two jobs.  We have to identify and keep good managers interested after we’ve figured out who they are.
And that often is a little different here, because I would say a majority of our managers are financially independent, so that they don't go to work because they are worried about putting kids through school or putting food on the table. So they have to have some reason to go to work aside from that.
They have to be treated fairly in terms of compensation, but they also have to figure it is better than playing golf every day or whatever it may be.
And, so that's one of the jobs we have and we basically attack that the same way — we look at what they do the same way we look at what we do.
We've got a wonderful group of shareholders. Before I ran this, I had a partnership. I had a great group of partners. And essentially, I like to be left alone to do what I did. I like to be judged on the scorecard at the end of the year rather than on every stroke, and not second guessed in a way that was inappropriate.
I like to have people who understood the environment in which I was operating.
And so the important thing we do with managers, generally, is to find the .400 hitters and then not tell them how to swing, as I put in the report.
The second thing we do is allocate capital. And aside from that, we play bridge. (Laughter)
Pretty much what happens at Berkshire.
So, with any of the managers you might name here, we try to make it interesting and fun for them to run their business. We try to have a compensation arrangement that's appropriate for the kind of business they’re in.
We have no company-wide compensation plan. We wouldn't dream of having some compensation expert or consultant come in and screw it up. (Laughter)
We try to — some businesses require a lot of capital that we're in, some require no capital. Some are easy businesses where good profit margins are a cinch to come by, but we're really paying for the extra beyond that. Some are very tough businesses to make money in.
And it would be crazy to have some huge framework that we try to place everybody in that — where one size would fit all.
People, generally, are compensated relating in some manner that relates to how their business does as opposed to — there's no reason to pay anybody based on how Berkshire does, because no one has responsibility for Berkshire except for Charlie and me.
And we try to make them responsible for their own units, compensated based on how those units do.
We try to understand the businesses they’re in, so we know what the difference between a good performance and a bad performance —
And that's about — that's how we work with people.
We've had terrific luck over the years in retaining the managers that we wanted to retain. I think, largely, it's because — particularly if they sell us a business — to a great extent, the next day they're running it just as they were the day before. And they're having as much fun running their business as I am running Berkshire.
Charlie?
CHARLIE MUNGER: Well, I've got nothing to add, but I think that concept of treating the other fellow the way you'd like to be treated if the roles were reversed — it's so simple, when you stop to think about it, but —
It's a rare evening when Ajit and Warren aren't talking once on the phone. It's more than a business relationship, at least it seems that way to me.
WARREN BUFFETT: Yeah, well, it is. It will stay that way, too.
CHARLIE MUNGER: And by the way, we like our businesses — our relationships — to be more than a business relationship
WARREN BUFFETT: Charlie and I are very — we basically — it's a luxury but it's a luxury that we should try to nurture — we get to work with people we like. And it makes life a lot simpler.
It probably helps in that goal of being the oldest living American, too. (Laughter)
CHARLIE MUNGER:  Yeah, and we tend to like people we admire. (Laughter)
WARREN BUFFETT: Yeah, who do we like that we don't admire, Charlie? (Laughter)
Start naming names. These people have names. (Laughter)
WARREN BUFFETT: Zone 2.
AUDIENCE MEMBER: My name is Peter Bevelin from Sweden.
How do you perceive Guinness long-term, economics growth-wise?
WARREN BUFFETT: Fitz — would you repeat that please, Fitz. What was it?  What firm growth-wise?
VOICE: Guinness.
AUDIENCE MEMBER: Guinness.
WARREN BUFFETT: Oh, Guinness.
I'm not as much of an expert on Guinness' products as Charlie is.
CHARLIE MUNGER: We approved that. (Laughter)
WARREN BUFFETT: You didn't hear him. He said, "I approved that."
I made the decision to buy Guinness and Guinness has — it's down somewhat from — actually, the price in pounds is about the same but the pound is at about $1.46 or -7 against an average of $1.80-something, so we've had a significant exchange loss on that.
The — Guinness' — despite the name — you know, the main product, of course, is scotch. And that's where most of the money is made, although they make good money in brewing.
But, distilling is the main business. And, you know, the usage of scotch, particularly in this country, the trends have not been strong at all, but that was true when we bought it, too.
There are some countries around the world where it's grown and there are certain countries where it's a huge prestige item.
I mean, in certain parts of the Far East, the more you pay for scotch, the better you think people think of you. Which I don't understand completely, but I hope it continues. (Laughter)

chapters.transcript_zh:
这个问题是关于衍生品的。
今天这个房间里，正好有写过这方面最佳文章的人。
大约一个月前，《财富》杂志上 Carol Loomis 写过一篇关于衍生品的文章，写得非常非常好，是目前为止最好的一篇。
我们现场还有一些来自所罗门、从事衍生品业务的人。
这是个非常宽泛的话题。
正如我们去年说的，我想当时有人问，90 年代最大的金融故事会是什么。
我们当时说，我们当然不知道，但如果非要选一个主题，那很可能就是衍生品。
原因在于，它们很容易被用来加上不同寻常规模的杠杆，
而且有时候连参与其中的人都没有完全理解它们。
只要你把无知和借来的钱放在一起，
就会产生相当有意思的后果，
尤其是当数字变得很大时。
最近宝洁公司的公告就让你们看到了这一点，
至于宝洁那些衍生品的细节，我并不清楚，但据我至少从
媒体报道中了解到，原本只是利率互换的东西，最后却变成了宝洁背上了看跌期权头寸。
chapters.transcript_en:
WARREN BUFFETT: Zone 2.
AUDIENCE MEMBER: My name is Peter Bevelin from Sweden.
How do you perceive Guinness long-term, economics growth-wise?
WARREN BUFFETT: Fitz — would you repeat that please, Fitz. What was it?  What firm growth-wise?
VOICE: Guinness.
AUDIENCE MEMBER: Guinness.
WARREN BUFFETT: Oh, Guinness.
I'm not as much of an expert on Guinness' products as Charlie is.
CHARLIE MUNGER: We approved that. (Laughter)
WARREN BUFFETT: You didn't hear him. He said, "I approved that."
I made the decision to buy Guinness and Guinness has — it's down somewhat from — actually, the price in pounds is about the same but the pound is at about $1.46 or -7 against an average of $1.80-something, so we've had a significant exchange loss on that.
The — Guinness' — despite the name — you know, the main product, of course, is scotch. And that's where most of the money is made, although they make good money in brewing.
But, distilling is the main business. And, you know, the usage of scotch, particularly in this country, the trends have not been strong at all, but that was true when we bought it, too.
There are some countries around the world where it's grown and there are certain countries where it's a huge prestige item.
I mean, in certain parts of the Far East, the more you pay for scotch, the better you think people think of you. Which I don't understand completely, but I hope it continues. (Laughter)
